message.content: Were working on our MCP server just now and I accidentally close the tab can we continue that session (

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

addedBlocks:
## claude.ai Linear
When passing string values to tools, send the content directly without escape sequences. For example, use real newlines in markdown content rather than literal backslash-n (\n) characters.

## claude.ai Supabase
Here are guidelines for using Supabase tools effectively:

- Before making schema changes, use `list_tables` to understand the existing structure
- When debugging issues, start with `get_logs` and `get_advisors` before making changes
- Use `get_project_url` and `get_publishable_api_key` when helping users configure client-side integrations

If you have access to a local development environment with a filesystem and shell:
- Install the Supabase agent skill for critical development and security guidance: `npx skills add supabase/agent-skills` (https://supabase.com/docs/guides/getting-started/ai-skills.md)
- Use the Supabase CLI (`supabase`) for local development workflows such as starting a local stack, managing migrations, and running edge functions locally (https://supabase.com/docs/guides/local-development.md)
- Prefer local development and testing before applying changes to a remote project

If you are running in a web-only or remote environment without filesystem or shell access:
- Rely on the MCP tools directly for all Supabase interactions
- Use `apply_migration` carefully, as changes go directly to the remote project

attachment.content:
- find-skills: Helps users discover and install agent skills when they ask questions like "how do I do X", "find a skill for X", "is there a skill that can...", or express interest in extending capabilities. This skill should be used when the user is looking for functionality that might exist as an installable skill.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Guidance for distinctive, intentional visual design when building new UI or reshaping an existing one. Helps with aesthetic direction, typography, and making choices that don't read as templated defaults.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

lastPrompt: Were working on our MCP server just now and I accidentally close the tab can we continue that session (

content.text: Short answer: I can't automatically reload the previous tab's conversation — each session starts fresh and I don't have that chat's context. But we can almost certainly reconstruct where you were. Let me look for traces of the MCP server work.

# Metrimap MCP server (scaffold)

The remote MCP server that exposes Metrimap's build/ingest tools to a user's own
agent (Claude, Codex). Design authority: product vault
`PRD/4. Product/3. MCP and Programmatic Building` (CVS-97). This doc covers the
scaffold shipped in **CVS-100** and the transport/deploy that remains.

## Layers

```
agent  ──MCP──▶  transport adapter (Streamable HTTP)   [deploy — this doc]
                    │  resolve auth per request  ───────  AuthContextResolver (CVS-99)
                    ▼
                 tool registry + dispatch  ─────────────  src/shared/lib/api/mcp  (CVS-100 ✓)
                    ▼
                 createMetrimapApi(client, userId)  ─────  src/shared/lib/api  (CVS-98 ✓, RLS-scoped)
                    ▼
                 Supabase (RLS)
```

## Shipped in CVS-100 — `src/shared/lib/api/mcp`

Transport-agnostic and unit-tested (no server/DB needed):

- **`registry.ts`** — the tool registry: each tool has a name, agent-facing
  description, Zod `inputSchema`, a `read`/`write` scope, and a handler that calls
  the CVS-98 API. `dispatchTool(name, args, ctx)` validates input, enforces scope,
  runs the handler, and raises **structured `McpToolError`s** (`invalid_input` /
  `unauthenticated` / `forbidden` / `not_found` / `internal`). `listTools()` feeds
  `tools/list`.

# Metrimap MCP server (scaffold)

The remote MCP server that exposes Metrimap's build/ingest tools to a user's own
agent (Claude, Codex). Design authority: product vault
`PRD/4. Product/3. MCP and Programmatic Building` (CVS-97). This doc covers the
scaffold shipped in **CVS-100** and the transport/deploy that remains.

## Layers

```
agent  ──MCP──▶  transport adapter (Streamable HTTP)   [deploy — this doc]
                    │  resolve auth per request  ───────  AuthContextResolver (CVS-99)
                    ▼
                 tool registry + dispatch  ─────────────  src/shared/lib/api/mcp  (CVS-100 ✓)
                    ▼
                 createMetrimapApi(client, userId)  ─────  src/shared/lib/api  (CVS-98 ✓, RLS-scoped)
                    ▼
                 Supabase (RLS)
```

## Shipped in CVS-100 — `src/shared/lib/api/mcp`

Transport-agnostic and unit-tested (no server/DB needed):

- **`registry.ts`** — the tool registry: each tool has a name, agent-facing
  description, Zod `inputSchema`, a `read`/`write` scope, and a handler that calls
  the CVS-98 API. `dispatchTool(name, args, ctx)` validates input, enforces scope,
  runs the handler, and raises **structured `McpToolError`s** (`invalid_input` /
  `unauthenticated` / `forbidden` / `not_found` / `internal`). `listTools()` feeds
  `tools/list`.
- **`authContext.ts`** — the **auth seam**: `McpAuthContext { userId, client, scopes }`
  and the `AuthContextResolver` type that **CVS-99** implements (OAuth token
  exchange / API-key lookup → a Clerk-authenticated, RLS-scoped client). Until
  then `unimplementedAuthResolver` refuses calls. The service-role key is never
  used.
- **`errors.ts`** — the structured error codes.

Tools wired (finalized in **CVS-101**): `list_canvases`, `get_tree`, `list_nodes`,
`list_relationships`, `create_canvas`, `update_canvas`, `delete_canvas`,
`create_metric`, `create_value`, `create_action`, `create_driver_node`,

---mcp-server tree---
mcp-server/.env.example
mcp-server/Dockerfile
mcp-server/README.md
mcp-server/deploy/.last-ip
mcp-server/deploy/Caddyfile
mcp-server/deploy/cloud-init.yaml
mcp-server/deploy/docker-compose.yml

mcp-server/fly.toml
mcp-server/package.json
mcp-server/server.ts
mcp-server/tsconfig.json

---provision.sh---
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Provision a Hetzner Cloud VPS for the Metrimap MCP server (idempotent).
# Reads ~/.metrimap-deploy.env (git-ignored): HCLOUD_TOKEN required.
# Creates an SSH key (kept on THIS machine so you retain access) + a server with
# cloud-init (Docker + firewall). Prints the server IP for your DNS record.
set -euo pipefail
HERE="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
ENV_FILE="${DEPLOY_ENV:-$HOME/.metrimap-deploy.env}"
[ -f "$ENV_FILE" ] || { echo "❌ Missing $ENV_FILE (see mcp-server/deploy/README.md)"; exit 1; }
set -a; . "$ENV_FILE"; set +a
: "${HCLOUD_TOKEN:?set HCLOUD_TOKEN in $ENV_FILE}"

SERVER_NAME="${SERVER_NAME:-metrimap-mcp}"
SERVER_TYPE="${SERVER_TYPE:-cpx22}"    # 2 vCPU / 4 GB. cpx12 = 1/2 (cheaper).
LOCATION="${LOCATION:-sin}"            # Singapore (x86 cpx/ccx only). nbg1/ash also fine.
IMAGE="${IMAGE:-ubuntu-24.04}"
KEY_PATH="${KEY_PATH:-$HOME/.ssh/metrimap_mcp}"
API="https://api.hetzner.cloud/v1"

# NB: no -f — we want error bodies so failures are legible.
hc() { curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer $HCLOUD_TOKEN" -H "Content-Type: application/json" "$@"; }
jget() { python3 -c "import sys,json;d=json.load(sys.stdin);print($1)"; }
jstr() { python3 -c 'import json,sys;print(json.dumps(sys.argv[1]))' "$1"; }

# 1) SSH key (generate locally if absent; upload to Hetzner if not there).
if [ ! -f "$KEY_PATH" ]; then
  mkdir -p "$(dirname "$KEY_PATH")"
  ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N '' -f "$KEY_PATH" -C "metrimap-mcp" >/dev/null
  echo "🔑 Generated SSH key at $KEY_PATH (keep it — it's your server access)."
fi
PUB="$(cat "$KEY_PATH.pub")"
key_id="$(hc "$API/ssh_keys?name=$SERVER_NAME" | jget 'd["ssh_keys"][0]["id"] if d.get("ssh_keys") else ""')"
if [ -z "$key_id" ]; then
  key_id="$(hc -X POST "$API/ssh_keys" -d "{\"name\":\"$SERVER_NAME\",\"public_key\":$(jstr "$PUB")}" | jget 'd["ssh_key"]["id"]')"
  echo "🔑 Uploaded SSH key to Hetzner (id $key_id)."
fi

# 2) Server (create if absent).
sid="$(hc "$API/servers?name=$SERVER_NAME" | jget 'd["servers"][0]["id"] if d.get("servers") else ""')"
if [ -z "$sid" ]; then
  echo "Creating $SERVER_NAME ($SERVER_TYPE @ $LOCATION, $IMAGE)…"
  USER_DATA="$(jstr "$(cat "$HERE/cloud-init.yaml")")"
  body="{\"name\":\"$SERVER_NAME\",\"server_type\":\"$SERVER_TYPE\",\"image\":\"$IMAGE\",\"location\":\"$LOCATION\",\"ssh_keys\":[$key_id],\"user_data\":$USER_DATA}"
  resp="$(hc -X POST "$API/servers" -d "$body")"
  sid="$(printf '%s' "$resp" | jget 'd.get("server",{}).get("id","")' 2>/dev/null || true)"
  if [ -z "$sid" ]; then
    echo "❌ Server create failed. Hetzner said:"
    printf '%s\n' "$resp" | python3 -m json.tool 2>/dev/null || printf '%s\n' "$resp"
    exit 1
  fi
  echo "🖥  Server created (id $sid). Waiting for it to boot…"
fi

# 3) Wait for a public IP + running status.
ip=""
for _ in $(seq 1 60); do
  line="$(hc "$API/servers/$sid" | jget 'd["server"]["status"]+"|"+((d["server"]["public_net"]["ipv4"] or {}).get("ip","") if d["server"].get("public_net") else "")')"
  status="${line%%|*}"; ip="${line#*|}"
  [ "$status" = "running" ] && [ -n "$ip" ] && break
  sleep 5
done
[ -n "$ip" ] || { echo "❌ No IP yet — check the Hetzner console."; exit 1; }

echo
echo "✅ Server up: $SERVER_NAME ($SERVER_TYPE @ $LOCATION)"
echo "   IP:  $ip"
echo "   SSH: ssh -i $KEY_PATH root@$ip"
echo
echo "Next: add DNS  →  ${MCP_DOMAIN:-mcp.canvasm.app}  A  $ip"
echo "SERVER_IP=$ip" > "$HERE/.last-ip"

---.last-ip---
SERVER_IP=5.223.49.175

content.text: That's a clean, well-understood failure — not a lost session. The Docker build dies at `npm install` because the root `package.json` has a `"prepare": "husky"` lifecycle script, and husky is a devDependency that `--omit=dev` doesn't install → `husky: not found` (exit 127). Let me look at the Dockerfile and fix it.

===== Dockerfile =====
# Build from the REPO ROOT:  docker build -f mcp-server/Dockerfile -t metrimap-mcp .
# The server imports shared code from src/, so it needs the app's root deps.
FROM node:22-slim
WORKDIR /app

# App runtime deps (the tool registry pulls supabase-js / zod / dagre from here).
COPY package.json package-lock.json* ./
RUN npm install --omit=dev --no-audit --no-fund

# App source the server imports + the server itself.
COPY tsconfig*.json ./
COPY src ./src
COPY mcp-server ./mcp-server

WORKDIR /app/mcp-server
RUN npm install --no-audit --no-fund

ENV PORT=8787
EXPOSE 8787
CMD ["npm", "start"]

content.text: The standard fix is `--ignore-scripts` on the root install — the root runtime deps (supabase-js/zod/dagre) have no build steps that need running, and it stops the `prepare`→husky hook from firing. Let me apply it.

content.text:
We're back on track — nothing from your closed session was lost, and I found and fixed the actual blocker.

## What happened
- Your MCP-server work lives in the **`metrimap-integrations` worktree** on branch **`mnadeemramli/mcp-vps-deploy`**. Everything was committed and pushed — the closed tab cost you no code.
- You'd already **provisioned the Hetzner VPS** (`5.223.49.175`) and applied the `mcp_resolve_api_key` SQL (the "Success. No rows returned" in your screenshot — that's expected for a `create function`).
- The deploy one-liner got all the way to `docker compose up -d --build` and died in the **Docker build**, not on infra.

## The failure
The build's root `npm install --omit=dev` still fired the root `package.json`'s `"prepare": "husky"` hook. `husky` is a devDependency, so `--omit=dev` never installed it → `husky: not found`, exit 127.

## The fix (done + committed as `a8740a5`)
Added `--ignore-scripts` to the root install in `mcp-server/Dockerfile`. That skips the `prepare`/husky hook; the runtime deps (supabase-js/zod/dagre) have no build steps to lose. The second install (`mcp-server/` itself) is untouched — it correctly keeps devDeps since `start` runs `tsx server.ts`.

## Re-run to finish the deploy
Your deploy command rsyncs the working tree, so just run the **exact same one-liner again**. It'll re-sync the fixed Dockerfile and rebuild. The `.env` write and Caddy `sed` are idempotent, so no harm in repeating.

Want me to also:
1. **Push the branch** (`git push`) so the fix is on origin, and/or
2. Walk through verifying `mcp.canvasm.app` end-to-end (DNS A record → Caddy TLS → `/health`) once the build succeeds?

One flag for step 2: I don't see confirmation that DNS `mcp.canvasm.app → 5.223.49.175` is set — Caddy needs that resolving before it can get a TLS cert. Have you added that A record yet?

message.content: yep always push to main, there's no point of doing this without pushing to main. then walk through the our mcp yep.

content.content:
===== mcp-server/README.md =====
# Metrimap MCP server (Node)

A tiny Node service that serves Metrimap's MCP tools over **Streamable HTTP**. It
just wires together the already-shipped, unit-tested pieces:

- tool registry + dispatch — `src/shared/lib/api/mcp` (CVS-100/101/102)
- API-key auth → per-user RLS-scoped client — `…/mcp/auth` (CVS-99)
- rate limit + payload caps + audit log — `…/mcp/guards` + `…/mcp/audit` (CVS-104)

`server.ts` only does HTTP + per-request auth. **Node was chosen over Vercel
functions** because MCP wants a long-lived Streamable-HTTP connection, which a
persistent Node process handles cleanly (and stays cheap on scale-to-zero hosts).

## What you need (the only manual bits)

1. **`SUPABASE_JWT_SECRET`** — Supabase dashboard → Project Settings → API →
   **JWT Secret**. This signs the short-lived per-user tokens. Server-only; never
   put it in the browser bundle or git.
2. `SUPABASE_URL` + `SUPABASE_ANON_KEY` (already public).
3. Before it works end to end, **merge + `db push` PR #69** (the
   `mcp_resolve_api_key` RPC) and #62 (audit log). Then generate an API key in
   the app (Settings → Connect your agent).

Copy `.env.example` → `.env` and fill those in.

## Run locally

```bash
# from the repo root — install app deps once (the server imports src/):
npm install
# then the server's own deps + start it:
cd mcp-server
npm install
cp .env.example .env    # edit it
npm start               # → http://localhost:8787  (GET /health, POST /mcp)
curl localhost:8787/health   # {"ok":true,...}
```

Point an agent at it:

```bash
claude mcp add --transport http metrimap http://localhost:8787/mcp \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer <your mk_live_ key>"
```

## Deploy to Fly.io (cheap, scales to zero)

```bash
# one-time
curl -L https://fly.io/install.sh | sh      # install flyctl
fly auth login
fly launch --no-deploy                       # creates the app (uses fly.toml)

# secrets (server-only)
fly secrets set \
  SUPABASE_URL=https://iqrclwolhookzzmiedun.supabase.co \
  SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=<anon key> \
  SUPABASE_JWT_SECRET=<jwt secret>

fly deploy                                   # builds mcp-server/Dockerfile
fly status                                   # note the *.fly.dev URL
```

Then set `VITE_MCP_URL=https://<app>.fly.dev/mcp` for the app's Connect page, or
map `mcp.canvasm.app` to the Fly app.

## Deploy anywhere else (Docker)

`Dockerfile` builds from the **repo root** (it needs `src/`):

```bash
docker build -f mcp-server/Dockerfile -t metrimap-mcp .
docker run -p 8787:8787 --env-file mcp-server/.env metrimap-mcp
```

Works the same on Render / Railway / a VPS — set the 3 env vars, expose 8787,
health-check `/health`.

## Notes

- **First run:** the `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` transport / `registerTool` API
  shifts a bit between versions. If `npm start` throws on an SDK call, pin the
  version in `package.json` and re-run — the two SDK touch-points are the
  `McpServer`/`registerTool` loop and the `StreamableHTTPServerTransport` in
  `server.ts`. Everything imported from `@/shared/lib/api/mcp` is version-stable
  and tested.
- **Scaling:** the rate limiter is in-memory (per instance). Fine for one
  machine; for multiple, back it with Postgres/Redis.
- **OAuth "Connect"** (sign-in instead of a key) is a follow-up — this server does
  the API-key path (CVS-99).

```bash
  SUPABASE_URL=https://iqrclwolhookzzmiedun.supabase.co \
  SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=<anon key> \
  SUPABASE_JWT_SECRET=<jwt secret>

fly deploy                                   # builds mcp-server/Dockerfile
fly status                                   # note the *.fly.dev URL
```

Then set `VITE_MCP_URL=https://<app>.fly.dev/mcp` for the app's Connect page, or
map `mcp.canvasm.app` to the Fly app.

## Deploy anywhere else (Docker)

`Dockerfile` builds from the **repo root** (it needs `src/`):

```bash
docker build -f mcp-server/Dockerfile -t metrimap-mcp .
docker run -p 8787:8787 --env-file mcp-server/.env metrimap-mcp
```

Works the same on Render / Railway / a VPS — set the 3 env vars, expose 8787,
health-check `/health`.

## Notes

- **First run:** the `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` transport / `registerTool` API
  shifts a bit between versions. If `npm start` throws on an SDK call, pin the
  version in `package.json` and re-run — the two SDK touch-points are the
  `McpServer`/`registerTool` loop and the `StreamableHTTPServerTransport` in
  `server.ts`. Everything imported from `@/shared/lib/api/mcp` is version-stable
  and tested.
- **Scaling:** the rate limiter is in-memory (per instance). Fine for one
  machine; for multiple, back it with Postgres/Redis.
- **OAuth "Connect"** (sign-in instead of a key) is a follow-up — this server does
  the API-key path (CVS-99).

===== deploy/docker-compose.yml =====
# Runs on the VPS at /opt/metrimap/mcp-server/deploy. Builds the server from the
# repo root (context ../..) and fronts it with Caddy for automatic HTTPS.
services:
  mcp:
    build:
      context: ../..
      dockerfile: mcp-server/Dockerfile
    env_file: .env # SUPABASE_URL / SUPABASE_ANON_KEY / SUPABASE_JWT_SECRET
    restart: unless-stopped
    expose:
      - "8787"

  caddy:
    image: caddy:2
    restart: unless-stopped
    depends_on:
      - mcp
    ports:
      - "80:80"
      - "443:443"
    volumes:
      - ./Caddyfile:/etc/caddy/Caddyfile:ro
      - caddy_data:/data
      - caddy_config:/config

volumes:
  caddy_data:
  caddy_config:

===== deploy/Caddyfile =====
# Automatic HTTPS (Let's Encrypt) once DNS points MCP_DOMAIN at this server.
# MCP_DOMAIN is substituted by deploy.sh (defaults to mcp.canvasm.app).
{$MCP_DOMAIN} {
	reverse_proxy mcp:8787
}

===== .env.example =====
# metrimap-mcp-server — copy to .env (git-ignored) and fill in.
# Server-only secrets — NEVER ship to the browser bundle.

SUPABASE_URL=https://iqrclwolhookzzmiedun.supabase.co
SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=<your Supabase anon/publishable key>

# The signer for per-user RLS tokens. Supabase dashboard →
# Project Settings → API → JWT Secret.
SUPABASE_JWT_SECRET=<your Supabase JWT secret>

PORT=8787

content.text:
Everything's on `main` now and the build blocker is fixed. Here's the full deploy walkthrough — picking up exactly where the closed tab left off.

## Where you are
- ✅ VPS provisioned (Hetzner, `5.223.49.175`)
- ✅ `mcp_resolve_api_key` RPC applied (your screenshot — "Success, no rows" is correct for a `create function`)
- ✅ Docker build blocker fixed (husky `prepare` hook) — on `main`
- ⬜ Container build + run on the box
- ⬜ HTTPS via Caddy (needs DNS)
- ⬜ Generate app API key + connect Claude

## Step 1 — Re-run your deploy one-liner (unchanged)
Your command rsyncs the **working tree** to the box and rebuilds, so it'll pick up the fixed `Dockerfile` automatically. Run the exact same one-liner again from `~/workspace/github.com/nadeemramli/metrimap-integrations`. It's idempotent (the `.env` write and Caddy `sed` just re-apply).

What it does, in order: wait for Docker on the box → `rsync` the repo to `/opt/metrimap` → write `deploy/.env` (Supabase URL/anon/JWT) → substitute `mcp.canvasm.app` into the Caddyfile → `docker compose up -d --build` → curl `localhost:8787/health`.

**Success looks like:** the build clears the old `[4/9] RUN npm install` step (that's what was failing), both `mcp` and `caddy` containers come up, and the final line prints `{"ok":true,...} ← server healthy`.

## Step 2 — The one thing that gates HTTPS: DNS
The health check in your one-liner only hits `localhost:8787`, so it'll pass even with no DNS. But **Caddy can't get a TLS cert until `mcp.canvasm.app` → `5.223.49.175` resolves publicly** (Let's Encrypt does an HTTP-01 challenge on port 80).

**Have you added that A record yet?** That's the deciding factor for whether Step 3 works on the first try.

## Step 3 — Verify end-to-end (once DNS is live)
```bash
curl https://mcp.canvasm.app/health      # → {"ok":true,"name":"metrimap-mcp",...}
```
If the cert isn't ready yet, check Caddy: `ssh -i ~/.ssh/metrimap_mcp root@5.223.49.175 'cd /opt/metrimap/mcp-server/deploy && docker compose logs caddy --tail 30'`.

## Step 4 — Connect an agent
1. In the app: **Settings → Connect your agent** → generate a key (`mk_live_…`). The app stores only its hash; the server resolves it via the `mcp_resolve_api_key` RPC you already applied.
2. Point Claude at it:
```bash
claude mcp add --transport http metrimap https://mcp.canvasm.app/mcp \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer mk_live_<your key>"
```
Then `claude mcp list` / call `list_canvases` to confirm the tools resolve under your RLS.

---

Two questions before you fire Step 1, so I can pre-empt the likely snags:

1. **Is the `mcp.canvasm.app` A record pointing at `5.223.49.175` yet?** (gates TLS)
2. **Does `~/.metrimap-deploy.env` have `SUPABASE_JWT_SECRET` set?** The one-liner pipes it into the box's `.env`; if it's blank the container will boot-loop with *"Missing env"* (server.ts exits if `SUPABASE_JWT_SECRET` is absent). Worth confirming since that secret is separate from the anon key.

Also — noting your workflow preference for next time:

content.text: Yes we've setup the mcp,[Image #3] is this correct? ,yes the metrimap have supabase jwt secret. What's that rerun the same deploy I don't get it?
[Image: source: /home/nadeemramli/.claude/image-cache/0c6415d0-0c00-4b81-bf75-b43856b1d88b/3.png]

Your DNS is **correct** — `mcp` A record → `5.223.49.175` (that's `mcp.canvasm.app`). TTL 60 is fine. That unblocks Caddy's TLS cert. ✅

## What "re-run the same deploy" means

That big multi-line command you pasted earlier (the one that ended in the `husky: not found` build error) **is** the deploy. It's not a script with a name — it's that one long chained command. Nothing about *it* changes. What changed is the **file it copies**: I fixed `mcp-server/Dockerfile` in your `metrimap-integrations` worktree.

Here's the mechanic that makes re-running work:

> The command does `rsync ... ./ root@$IP:/opt/metrimap/` — it copies your **local worktree** up to the server, then runs `docker compose up -d --build` **on the server**. Last time it copied the *broken* Dockerfile, so the build failed. Now the worktree has the *fixed* Dockerfile, so the same command copies the fix up and the build gets past the step that was dying.

So: **paste the exact same block again.** No edits needed.

## The command (unchanged — copy/paste into your terminal)

```bash
cd ~/workspace/github.com/nadeemramli/metrimap-integrations && \
set -a && . ~/.metrimap-deploy.env && set +a && \
IP=5.223.49.175 && KEY=~/.ssh/metrimap_mcp && SSHOPT="-i $KEY -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new" && \
until ssh $SSHOPT root@$IP 'command -v docker' >/dev/null 2>&1; do echo "waiting for docker…"; sleep 5; done && \
rsync -az --delete -e "ssh $SSHOPT" --exclude node_modules --exclude .git --exclude dist ./ root@$IP:/opt/metrimap/ && \
printf 'SUPABASE_URL=%s\nSUPABASE_ANON_KEY=%s\nSUPABASE_JWT_SECRET=%s\nPORT=8787\n' \
  "${SUPABASE_URL:-https://iqrclwolhookzzmiedun.supabase.co}" "$SUPABASE_ANON_KEY" "$SUPABASE_JWT_SECRET" \
  | ssh $SSHOPT root@$IP 'cat > /opt/metrimap/mcp-server/deploy/.env' && \
ssh $SSHOPT root@$IP "sed -i 's/{\$MCP_DOMAIN}/mcp.canvasm.app/' /opt/metrimap/mcp-server/deploy/Caddyfile && cd /opt/metrimap/mcp-server/deploy && docker compose up -d --build" && \
sleep 5 && ssh $SSHOPT root@$IP 'curl -fsS localhost:8787/health && echo " ← server healthy"'
```

**This time you should see:** the `[4/9] RUN npm install` step complete instead of erroring, both containers start, and a final line:
```
{"ok":true,"name":"metrimap-mcp","version":"0.1.0"} ← server healthy
```

That last line = the server is up on the box. Then, since DNS is live, the public URL should work within a minute (Caddy fetches the cert on first request):
```bash
curl https://mcp.canvasm.app/health
```

---

I can't run this one for me on your behalf reliably (it SSHes to your box using the key on this machine, and the `sleep` calls get blocked in my sandboxed shell), so it's best pasted into your own terminal — or type it here prefixed with `!` to run it in-session.

Go ahead and run it, then paste the output (or just tell me it printed "server healthy") and we'll do the Claude connect step.

content.text:
🎉 **The build worked** — look at step `#9 [4/9] RUN npm install --omit=dev ... --ignore-scripts → DONE`. That's the exact step that was failing before; the husky fix is confirmed. Both containers built and started (`deploy-mcp-1 Started`, `deploy-caddy-1 Started`).

## The `localhost:8787` error is a red herring in the one-liner

That final `curl localhost:8787/health` was **always going to fail** — it's a bug in the check, not your server. Look at the compose file:

```yaml
mcp:
    expose:
      - "8787"      # ← internal to the Docker network only, NOT published to the host
caddy:
    ports:
      - "80:80"
      - "443:443"   # ← only Caddy is reachable from outside
```

The `mcp` container exposes 8787 **only inside the Docker network**, where Caddy reaches it as `mcp:8787`. It's deliberately *not* published to the host, so `curl localhost:8787` on the box can't see it. The real entry point is Caddy on 443. So that last line testing `localhost:8787` is just checking the wrong door.

## Test the right door instead

Since your DNS is live, hit the public URL:

```bash
curl https://mcp.canvasm.app/health
```

Expected: `{"ok":true,"name":"metrimap-mcp","version":"0.1.0"}`. First hit may pause a few seconds while Caddy fetches the Let's Encrypt cert — if it errors on the cert, wait ~30s and retry.

And confirm the container is actually running (not crash-looping on a missing env var):

```bash
ssh -i ~/.ssh/metrimap_mcp root@5.223.49.175 \
  'cd /opt/metrimap/mcp-server/deploy && docker compose ps && echo "--- mcp logs ---" && docker compose logs mcp --tail 30'
```

**What to look for in the logs:**
- ✅ `metrimap-mcp v0.1.0 listening on :8787` → server is healthy, we move to connecting Claude.
- ❌ `Missing env. Required: SUPABASE_URL, SUPABASE_ANON_KEY, SUPABASE_JWT_SECRET.` → one of those didn't make it into `/opt/metrimap/mcp-server/deploy/.env` (most likely `SUPABASE_JWT_SECRET` was empty in `~/.metrimap-deploy.env`), and the container exits. Easy fix if so.

Paste the output of those two commands and we'll either connect Claude or fix the env.

content.text:
Fully live. ✅ Everything checks out:
- `https://mcp.canvasm.app/health` → `{"ok":true,...}` — **public HTTPS works, Caddy got the cert**
- Both containers `Up 2 minutes`
- mcp logs: `metrimap-mcp v0.1.0 listening on :8787` — no missing-env crash

Your MCP server is deployed and serving at **`https://mcp.canvasm.app/mcp`**.

Now the last step — connecting an agent with an API key. Before you generate one, let me verify the auth path end-to-end, because this is the one spot where a mismatch (app hashes the key differently than the server looks it up) would silently reject you. Let me check the key-generation and lookup code.

input.command: cd /home/nadeemramli/workspace/github.com/nadeemramli/metrimap-integrations && echo "===== apiKeyResolver.ts =====" && cat src/shared/lib/api/mcp/auth/apiKeyResolver.ts

cd /home/nadeemramli/workspace/github.com/nadeemramli/metrimap-integrations && echo "===== resolve_api_key SQL =====" && cat supabase/migrations/20260704160000_mcp_resolve_api_key.sql && echo "" && echo "===== app-side key gen (grep) =====" && grep -rn "api_keys\|mk_live\|key_hash\|createApiKey\|generateApiKey" src/features/settings src/shared/lib/supabase/services 2>/dev/null | head -30
input.description: Read resolve SQL and find app-side key generation

content.text: App stores `key_hash = sha256hex(key)`; the server looks up `hashApiKey(key)`. Those must produce identical strings or auth silently fails. Let me confirm both are the same SHA-256 hex.

input.command: cd /home/nadeemramli/workspace/github.com/nadeemramli/metrimap-integrations && echo "===== jwt.ts hashApiKey =====" && sed -n '1,60p' src/shared/lib/api/mcp/auth/jwt.ts | grep -n "hashApiKey" -A 12 && echo "===== apiKeys.ts sha256hex =====" && sed -n '1,55p' src/shared/lib/supabase/services/apiKeys.ts

lastPrompt: {"ok":true,"name":"metrimap-mcp","version":"0.1.0"} . nadeemramli@Nadeems-Computer:~/workspace/github.com/nadeemramli/metrimap-integrations$ ssh -i ~/.ssh/metrimap_mcp root@5.223.49.175 \   'cd /opt/m…

content.text:
Auth path is **verified consistent** — no mismatch:

- **App** (`apiKeys.ts`): generates `mk_live_<hex>`, stores `key_hash = SHA-256 hex(key)` + `created_by` + `workspace_id`.
- **Server** (`jwt.ts` → `apiKeyResolver.ts`): computes `hashApiKey(key)` — byte-identical SHA-256 hex — and calls the `mcp_resolve_api_key` RPC.
- **RPC**: matches `key_hash` exactly (needs ≥32 chars; a 64-char hash clears it), returns the owner, mints a per-user JWT → RLS-scoped client.

So a key generated in the app will resolve on the server. Green light.

## Final step — connect Claude

1. **Generate a key** in the app: **Settings → Connect your agent** (`ConnectAgentPage`), click generate, copy the `mk_live_…` key (shown once). *Note: this reads/writes prod Supabase, so use your deployed app or a local dev instance pointed at prod — either works.*

2. **Add the server to Claude Code:**
```bash
claude mcp add --transport http metrimap https://mcp.canvasm.app/mcp \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer mk_live_<your key>"
```

3. **Verify the tools resolve under your RLS:**
```bash
claude mcp list          # metrimap should show ✓ connected
```
Then in a Claude session, call `list_canvases` — it should return your canvases (proof the whole chain works: key → hash → RPC → JWT → RLS query).

**If `list` shows the server but a tool call returns `unauthenticated`:** the key didn't resolve — almost always a copy/paste slip on the key, or the key was generated against a different Supabase project than the server's `SUPABASE_URL` (`iqrclwolhookzzmiedun`). The server logs will show it: `docker compose logs mcp --tail 20`.

Let me record this deployment so it's not lost next session:

name: mcp-server-deployed
description: "Metrimap MCP server is LIVE at mcp.canvasm.app on a Hetzner VPS; deploy runbook + auth chain"
metadata:
  type: project
---

The Metrimap MCP server (CVS-99 auth + CVS-100 Node server + CVS-104 hardening) is **deployed and live** as of 2026-07-05.

- **Public endpoint:** `https://mcp.canvasm.app/mcp` (health: `GET /health` → `{"ok":true,...}`).
- **Host:** Hetzner VPS `5.223.49.175` (SSH `ssh -i ~/.ssh/metrimap_mcp root@5.223.49.175`). DNS `mcp` A-record → that IP (Cloudflare/registrar, TTL 60).
- **Stack on the box:** `docker compose` at `/opt/metrimap/mcp-server/deploy` — `mcp` container (node, `expose 8787` internal only) behind a `caddy:2` container that publishes 80/443 and does automatic Let's Encrypt TLS. `curl localhost:8787` on the host FAILS by design (not published); test via the public URL or `docker compose logs mcp`.
- **Code:** `mcp-server/` (server.ts = thin HTTP/Streamable-HTTP adapter) wired over the tested registry in `src/shared/lib/api/mcp/`. Lives in the `../metrimap-integrations` worktree; all merged to `main` (`de17538`). See [[integrations-lane-worktree-2026-07]].

**Deploy = one chained command** (not a named script): `cd ~/workspace/.../metrimap-integrations` → `. ~/.metrimap-deploy.env` → wait-for-docker → `rsync ./` to `/opt/metrimap` → write `deploy/.env` (SUPABASE_URL/ANON_KEY/JWT_SECRET) → `sed` MCP_DOMAIN into Caddyfile → `docker compose up -d --build`. It rsyncs the **working tree**, so to redeploy you just re-run it after editing files. `~/.metrimap-deploy.env` (git-ignored) holds HCLOUD_TOKEN + the Supabase secrets; provisioning script is `mcp-server/deploy/provision.sh`.

**Gotcha fixed:** the Docker build needs `--ignore-scripts` on the root `npm install --omit=dev` (Dockerfile) — else the root `"prepare":"husky"` hook runs husky (a devDep that --omit=dev skips) → `husky: not found` exit 127.

**Auth chain (verified consistent):** app `createApiKey` (`services/apiKeys.ts`) makes `mk_live_<hex>`, stores `key_hash = sha256hex(key)` + `created_by`/`workspace_id` → server `hashApiKey` (same SHA-256 hex) → `mcp_resolve_api_key` SECURITY-DEFINER RPC (migration 20260704160000, applied to prod; anon-executable by design) returns owner + bumps last_used_at → mints Supabase JWT → RLS-scoped client. Connect: `claude mcp add --transport http metrimap https://mcp.canvasm.app/mcp --header "Authorization: Bearer mk_live_..."`. Generate the key in-app: Settings → Connect your agent (`ConnectAgentPage`).

Still follow-up: OAuth "Connect" sign-in (key path only for now); in-memory rate limiter is per-instance.

lastPrompt: [Image #6] Seems like it's working, but Yep, Canvasm is set up as a connector, but calling it just now returned an authentication error — it's missing an API key, so it looks like the connection isn't…

content.text:
That screenshot tells us exactly what's wrong — and it's good news: the connection and tool discovery **work** (Claude sees all 17 tools). The problem is purely auth, and it's a known limitation, not a bug in your deploy.

## What's happening

Look at *when* it fails: **listing** tools works, but **calling** one says "missing an API key." That's by design in `server.ts` — auth is resolved lazily, only when a tool actually runs:

- `tools/list` → no auth needed → succeeds (that's your 17 tools).
- any tool call → `resolveAuth` looks for an `Authorization: Bearer mk_live_…` header → **none present** → `unauthenticated: Missing API key`.

The claude.ai **Connectors UI** (that's what your screenshot is) has no field to attach a static API key. It only authenticates via **OAuth** — and our server doesn't implement OAuth yet. The README even calls this out: *"OAuth 'Connect' (sign-in instead of a key) is a follow-up — this server does the API-key path."* So the web connector connects, lists tools, but has no credential to send on calls.

## Two ways forward

**Option A — works right now: connect via Claude Code CLI**, which *can* attach the header:

1. Generate a key in your app: **Settings → Connect your agent** → copy the `mk_live_…`.
2. 
```bash
claude mcp add --transport http metrimap https://mcp.canvasm.app/mcp \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer mk_live_<your key>"
```
3. In a Claude Code session, call `list_canvases`. This path is fully supported by the server as deployed — no code changes.

*(Claude Desktop can also do this via a headers entry in its MCP config; the claude.ai web Connectors panel in your screenshot cannot.)*

**Option B — makes the web connector in your screenshot work: implement OAuth** on the server (the documented CVS follow-up). This is the real fix if you want teammates to add "Canvasm" from claude.ai/Desktop with a sign-in button instead of pasting a key. It's a meaningful chunk of work — the MCP auth handshake (401 + `WWW-Authenticate` → authorization-server metadata → token exchange), plus wiring it to Clerk.

One small thing to double-check regardless: the connector URL should be `https://mcp.canvasm.app/**mcp**`, not `/health` (the screenshot shows `/health`). Since tools listed, the MCP URL is probably already right and `/health` is just the check URL it displays — but worth confirming.

Which do you want?

I've found comprehensive information about MCP server OAuth 2.1 authorization specifications. Here's what the search revealed:

## Key OAuth 2.1 Requirements for MCP Servers

MCP servers MUST implement OAuth 2.0 Protected Resource Metadata (RFC9728). MCP clients MUST use OAuth 2.0 Protected Resource Metadata for authorization server discovery.

## Authorization Server Metadata

Authorization servers MUST provide OAuth 2.0 Authorization Server Metadata (RFC8414), and MCP clients MUST use the OAuth 2.0 Authorization Server Metadata.

## Dynamic Client Registration

MCP clients and authorization servers SHOULD support the OAuth 2.0 Dynamic Client Registration Protocol RFC7591 to allow MCP clients to obtain OAuth client IDs without user interaction.

## Protected Resource Metadata Discovery

MCP servers MUST use the HTTP header WWW-Authenticate when returning a 401 Unauthorized to indicate the location of the resource server metadata URL, and MCP clients MUST be able to parse WWW-Authenticate headers and respond appropriately to HTTP 401 Unauthorized responses from the MCP server.

## Claude Connector Support

Claude and ChatGPT now accept remote MCP connectors: paste your server URL; if the server requires auth, the host walks an OAuth flow.

## Complete Authorization Flow

The full OAuth 2.1 flow as it applies to MCP servers includes metadata discovery, dynamic client registration, PKCE-protected authorization, and token lifecycle management.

The specification ensures secure multi-client, multi-user authorization while maintaining compatibility with various MCP clients including Claude Desktop, Cursor, and other agents.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
I now have a complete picture of the auth surface. Here is the structured map.

# Metrimap MCP Auth Surface — Complete Map

All paths under the worktree `/home/nadeemramli/workspace/github.com/nadeemramli/metrimap-integrations` (branch `mnadeemramli/mcp-vps-deploy`).

## 1. Runnable MCP server — `mcp-server/server.ts`

HTTP wiring and auth flow:

- **HTTP server construction:** `createServer(...)` at `server.ts:123`. Routes: `GET /health` or `/` → JSON health (`:125-129`); non-POST → 405 (`:130-134`); everything else treated as an MCP POST (`:136-145`). Listens on `PORT` (default 8787) at `:155`.
- **Env / resolver setup:** requires `SUPABASE_URL`, `SUPABASE_ANON_KEY`, `SUPABASE_JWT_SECRET` (`:31-43`). One anon client for key lookups (`:46`). The API-key resolver is built once at `:47-52` via `createApiKeyAuthResolver({ jwtSecret, supabaseUrl, anonKey, lookupKey: supabaseApiKeyLookup(anon) })`.
- **Per-request auth resolution:** `buildMcpServer(reqHeaders)` at `:82`. Auth is resolved lazily and cached once per request via `getCtx()` (`:85-88`), which calls `resolveAuth({ headers: reqHeaders })`. Headers are lowercased in `headersOf()` (`:57-63`) before being handed in at `:137`.
- **Where the Bearer header is actually read:** not in `server.ts` — it delegates to the resolver. The extraction happens in `apiKeyResolver.ts:61-69` (`extractApiKey`, reads `authorization` / `Authorization` Bearer, falls back to `x-api-key`).
- **Where auth runs per tool call / where failures are raised:** inside each tool handler at `:100-118`. `await getCtx()` (`:102`) throws `McpToolError('unauthenticated', …)` if no/invalid key. The catch at `:109-116` maps `McpToolError.code` (else `'internal'`) into an `isError` MCP response (`code: message`).
- **Dispatch call:** `dispatchTool(t.name, args, authed, { rateLimiter, maxPayloadBytes: DEFAULT_MAX_PAYLOAD_BYTES, audit: supabaseAuditSink(authed.client) })` at `:103-107`.

**Exact OAuth hook point:** the single seam is `getCtx()` / the `resolveAuth` assignment at `server.ts:47-52` + `:85-88`. Today it is hardwired to `createApiKeyAuthResolver`. An OAuth bearer path slots in by making resolution dispatch on the token shape (see integration point at the end). The `.well-known` / `/authorize` / `/token` / DCR endpoints would additionally need new branches in the `createServer` request router (`server.ts:123-145`), which currently only handles `/health` and MCP POST.

## 2. The auth seam — `src/shared/lib/api/mcp/`

### `authContext.ts` (the seam contract)
- `McpScope = 'read' | 'write'` (`:10`).
- `McpAuthContext` (`:12-19`): `{ userId: string; client: SupabaseClient<Database>; scopes?: McpScope[] }` — RLS-scoped client, never service-role.
- `McpRequestLike` (`:22-24`): `{ headers: Record<string, string | undefined> }`.
- `AuthContextResolver` (`:27-29`): `(req: McpRequestLike) => Promise<McpAuthContext>` — **this is the type both API-key and OAuth resolvers must implement.**
- `unimplementedAuthResolver` (`:35-40`): placeholder that throws `McpToolError('unauthenticated', …)`. Exported but not used by the running server (server uses the real API-key resolver).

### `auth/apiKeyResolver.ts`
- `ApiKeyLookup` type (`:17-19`): `keyHash → { userId, orgId } | null`.
- `supabaseApiKeyLookup(anonClient)` (`:27-45`): calls RPC `mcp_resolve_api_key({ p_key_hash })`, maps `row.user_id`/`row.workspace_id` → `{ userId, orgId }`.
- `extractApiKey` (`:61-69`): reads `Authorization: Bearer <key>`, else `x-api-key`.
- `createApiKeyAuthResolver(opts)` (`:71-105`): the resolver factory. Flow: extract key → `hashApiKey(key)` → `opts.lookupKey(hash)` → `mintSupabaseJwt({ sub: userId, orgId }, jwtSecret, tokenTtlSec)` (`:94-98`) → build a Supabase client with `Authorization: Bearer <jwt>` (`:74-80`) → return `{ userId, client, scopes: opts.scopes ?? ['read','write'] }` (`:99-103`). Throws `McpToolError('unauthenticated', …)` at `:85` (missing) and `:92` (invalid/revoked).

### `auth/jwt.ts`
- `hashApiKey(key)` (`:20-23`): SHA-256 hex (matches `apiKeys.ts` convention).
- `SupabaseJwtInput` (`:25-30`): `{ sub, orgId? }`.
- `mintSupabaseJwt(input, secret, ttlSec=300)` (`:36-64`): HS256 via Web Crypto. Claims (`:44-52`): `sub`, `role: 'authenticated'`, `aud: 'authenticated'`, `iss: 'metrimap-mcp'`, `iat`, `exp = now + ttlSec` (default 5 min); and `o: { id: orgId }` only when `orgId` present (`:52`). Throws if secret missing (`:41`).
- `decodeJwtPayload` (`:67-71`): decode-only, no verify (tests/debug).
- **Claim → RLS mapping (confirmed):** `sub` → `public.requesting_user_id()` (`supabase/migrations/20250811100849_remote_schema.sql:127-138`, reads `request.jwt.claims::json->>'sub'`); `o.id` → `public.requesting_org_id()` (`supabase/migrations/20260629150000_workspace_org_scoping.sql:17-20`, reads `...->'o'->>'id'`).

### `index.ts` (module exports)
Barrel at `index.ts:4-38`: registry (`TOOLS`, `listTools`, `dispatchTool`, `McpTool`, `DispatchGuards`), errors (`McpToolError`, `McpErrorCode`), guards (`RateLimiter`, `enforcePayloadSize`, `enforceRateLimit`, `DEFAULT_MAX_PAYLOAD_BYTES`), audit (`noopAuditSink`, `supabaseAuditSink`, `AuditSink`, `McpAuditEntry`), authContext (`unimplementedAuthResolver`, `McpAuthContext`, `McpScope`, `McpRequestLike`, `AuthContextResolver`), and auth (`createApiKeyAuthResolver`, `supabaseApiKeyLookup`, `ApiKeyAuthOptions`, `ApiKeyLookup`, `mintSupabaseJwt`, `hashApiKey`, `decodeJwtPayload`).

### `errors.ts`
`McpErrorCode` (`:3-10`): `invalid_input | unauthenticated | forbidden | not_found | rate_limited | payload_too_large | internal`. `McpToolError extends Error` with `code` + optional `details` (`:12-21`).

### `guards.ts` + `audit.ts` (wrap dispatch)
- `RateLimiter` token bucket (`guards.ts:9-31`); server uses `new RateLimiter(120, 2)` = 120 burst, 2/s, keyed per user (`server.ts:55`; keyed by `ctx.userId` in dispatch).
- `DEFAULT_MAX_PAYLOAD_BYTES = 2_000_000` (`guards.ts:35`); `enforcePayloadSize` (`:37-53`) → `payload_too_large`; `enforceRateLimit` (`:55-63`) → `rate_limited`.
- `audit.ts`: `McpAuditEntry` (`:9-16`), `AuditSink` (`:18-20`), `supabaseAuditSink(client)` (`:26-41`) inserts into `mcp_audit_log` via the caller's RLS client (best-effort). These are injected into dispatch, not wrapped around it externally.

### `registry.ts` — dispatch
- `dispatchTool(name, rawArgs, ctx: McpAuthContext, guards: DispatchGuards = {})` (`:228-288`). Order: payload cap (`:238`) → rate limit keyed on `ctx.userId` (`:239`) → tool lookup / `not_found` (`:241-242`) → **scope check** `tool.scope === 'write' && ctx.scopes && !ctx.scopes.includes('write')` → `forbidden` (`:244-246`) → Zod parse / `invalid_input` (`:264-274`) → run `tool.handler(args, ctx)` (`:277`) → audit ok/error (`:248-262`).
- Each handler builds the API via `api(ctx) = createMetrimapApi(ctx.client, ctx.userId)` (`:41`), i.e. the auth context's client + userId drive all DB access under RLS. 18 tools registered (`TOOLS`, `:47-198`), tagged `read`/`write`.

## 3. Existing OAuth scaffolding

**No functional OAuth code exists — only a typed seam, disabled UI, and docs.** Nothing matched for `PKCE`, `code_verifier`, `WWW-Authenticate`, `/.well-known`, `protected-resource`, `authorization_server`, or a `/register` (DCR) endpoint in code. What exists:

- **Seam/placeholder:** `authContext.ts:26-40` (`AuthContextResolver` type + `unimplementedAuthResolver` referencing "CVS-99 OAuth Connect").
- **Disabled UI:** `ConnectAgentPage.tsx:221-240` — an "Connect with sign-in (OAuth)" section with a "Soon" badge and a `disabled` Connect button.
- **Design doc only:** `docs/features/mcp-server.md:135-142` ("OAuth 'Connect' — follow-up") describes the intended design: an OAuth 2.1 authorization server with dynamic client registration + `/authorize` + `/token`, backed by Clerk sign-in, needing a Clerk OAuth application. Explicitly states it "resolves the same way — a bearer token → user → `mintSupabaseJwt` → RLS-scoped client — so it slots into the same seam."
- Other mentions are narrative only: `docs/features/mcp-connect.md:15`, `mcp-server/README.md:89`, `apiKeyResolver.ts:1-5`, `index.ts:31`.

## 4. App-side API key generation

### `src/shared/lib/supabase/services/apiKeys.ts`
- `randomKey()` (`:25-30`): `mk_live_<48 hex chars>` (24 random bytes).
- `sha256hex()` (`:18-23`): same SHA-256 hex convention as `jwt.ts:hashApiKey` (so lookups line up).
- `createApiKey(name, client)` (`:43-58`): generates key, stores `{ name, key_hash, key_prefix }` (prefix = first 12 chars, `:50`), returns full key **once** (`:57`). No scopes stored.
- `listApiKeys` (`:32-40`) selects `id, name, key_prefix, created_at, last_used_at`; `deleteApiKey` (`:60-64`). All go through `resolveClient(client)` (user RLS client).

### `src/features/settings/pages/ConnectAgentPage.tsx`
"Connect your agent" UI (route `/settings/connect`). Shows: MCP URL (`MCP_URL`, default `https://mcp.canvasm.app/mcp`, overridable via `VITE_MCP_URL`, `:24-26`); a one-time key generator (`generateKey`, `:99-110`); a copy-paste `claude mcp add --transport http metrimap <URL> --header "Authorization: Bearer <key>"` snippet (`:113-117`); claude.ai connector instructions (`:198-208`); a raw `Authorization: Bearer` header for other clients (`:210-219`); the disabled OAuth section (`:221-240`); and a key-count/last-used status (`:242-262`).

## 5. `api_keys` schema + related migrations

### `supabase/migrations/20260630120000_api_keys.sql`
Table `public.api_keys` (`:8-17`): `id uuid PK`, `workspace_id text DEFAULT requesting_org_id()`, `created_by text NOT NULL DEFAULT requesting_user_id()`, `name text`, `key_prefix text`, `key_hash text NOT NULL UNIQUE`, `created_at`, `last_used_at`. **No `scopes` column** (confirmed by grep — per-key scopes are noted as future CVS-89). RLS (`:19-31`): select/insert/delete all gated on `created_by = requesting_user_id()`. There is **no UPDATE policy**, so the `last_used_at` bump must go through the SECURITY DEFINER RPC below.

### `supabase/migrations/20260704160000_mcp_resolve_api_key.sql`
`public.mcp_resolve_api_key(p_key_hash text)` (`:11-30`): `SECURITY DEFINER`, returns `(user_id text, workspace_id text)`. Rejects hashes < 32 chars (`:18`), bumps `last_used_at` (`:22`), returns owner `created_by` + `workspace_id` for exact hash match (`:24-28`). Granted EXECUTE to `anon, authenticated, service_role` (`:33`) so the pre-auth anon lookup in `apiKeyResolver.ts` works without a service-role key.

### `supabase/migrations/20260704150000_mcp_audit_log.sql`
`public.mcp_audit_log` (`:10-19`): `id`, `user_id text DEFAULT requesting_user_id()`, `tool_name`, `scope`, `outcome CHECK IN ('ok','error')`, `error_code`, `duration_ms`, `created_at`. Index on `(user_id, created_at DESC)` (`:21-22`). RLS append-only — select/insert gated on `user_id = requesting_user_id()`, no update/delete (`:27-30`).

---

## Clearest OAuth integration point

**The single seam is the `AuthContextResolver` at `mcp-server/server.ts:47-52` (resolver construction) consumed at `server.ts:85-88` (`getCtx`).** Both auth methods produce the same `McpAuthContext` and everything downstream (`dispatchTool`, guards, audit, RLS) is auth-method-agnostic.

The cleanest slot-in is a **composite resolver** that inspects the extracted bearer token and branches:
- Opaque `mk_live_…` prefix (or hash-lookup hit) → existing `createApiKeyAuthResolver` path (`apiKeyResolver.ts:71-105`).
- A JWT/OAuth access token → new OAuth resolver that verifies the token, extracts the Clerk user id (+ org), and calls the **already-shared** `mintSupabaseJwt({ sub, orgId }, SUPABASE_JWT_SECRET)` (`jwt.ts:36-64`) to produce the same RLS-scoped client.

Both branches return `McpAuthContext`, so no change is needed in `dispatchTool`, guards, audit, the registry, or any tool. The only other work is HTTP-surface: add the OAuth 2.1 metadata/endpoints (`/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource`, `/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server`, `/authorize`, `/token`, `/register`) as new branches in the `createServer` router (`server.ts:123-145`), and emit a `WWW-Authenticate` header on the `unauthenticated` failure so MCP clients can discover the auth server. Per-key/per-token scopes would need a new `scopes` column on `api_keys` (currently absent) if you want to move beyond the default `['read','write']`.

Note: I did not modify anything — this is a read-only report.

I now have a complete picture of the auth surface. Here is the structured map.

# Metrimap MCP Auth Surface — Complete Map

All paths under the worktree `/home/nadeemramli/workspace/github.com/nadeemramli/metrimap-integrations` (branch `mnadeemramli/mcp-vps-deploy`).

## 1. Runnable MCP server — `mcp-server/server.ts`

HTTP wiring and auth flow:

- **HTTP server construction:** `createServer(...)` at `server.ts:123`. Routes: `GET /health` or `/` → JSON health (`:125-129`); non-POST → 405 (`:130-134`); everything else treated as an MCP POST (`:136-145`). Listens on `PORT` (default 8787) at `:155`.
- **Env / resolver setup:** requires `SUPABASE_URL`, `SUPABASE_ANON_KEY`, `SUPABASE_JWT_SECRET` (`:31-43`). One anon client for key lookups (`:46`). The API-key resolver is built once at `:47-52` via `createApiKeyAuthResolver({ jwtSecret, supabaseUrl, anonKey, lookupKey: supabaseApiKeyLookup(anon) })`.
- **Per-request auth resolution:** `buildMcpServer(reqHeaders)` at `:82`. Auth is resolved lazily and cached once per request via `getCtx()` (`:85-88`), which calls `resolveAuth({ headers: reqHeaders })`. Headers are lowercased in `headersOf()` (`:57-63`) before being handed in at `:137`.
- **Where the Bearer header is actually read:** not in `server.ts` — it delegates to the resolver. The extraction happens in `apiKeyResolver.ts:61-69` (`extractApiKey`, reads `authorization` / `Authorization` Bearer, falls back to `x-api-key`).
- **Where auth runs per tool call / where failures are raised:** inside each tool handler at `:100-118`. `await getCtx()` (`:102`) throws `McpToolError('unauthenticated', …)` if no/invalid key. The catch at `:109-116` maps `McpToolError.code` (else `'internal'`) into an `isError` MCP response (`code: message`).
- **Dispatch call:** `dispatchTool(t.name, args, authed, { rateLimiter, maxPayloadBytes: DEFAULT_MAX_PAYLOAD_BYTES, audit: supabaseAuditSink(authed.client) })` at `:103-107`.

**Exact OAuth hook point:** the single seam is `getCtx()` / the `resolveAuth` assignment at `server.ts:47-52` + `:85-88`. Today it is hardwired to `createApiKeyAuthResolver`. An OAuth bearer path slots in by making resolution dispatch on the token shape (see integration point at the end). The `.well-known` / `/authorize` / `/token` / DCR endpoints would additionally need new branches in the `createServer` request router (`server.ts:123-145`), which currently only handles `/health` and MCP POST.

## 2. The auth seam — `src/shared/lib/api/mcp/`

### `authContext.ts` (the seam contract)
- `McpScope = 'read' | 'write'` (`:10`).
- `McpAuthContext` (`:12-19`): `{ userId: string; client: SupabaseClient<Database>; scopes?: McpScope[] }` — RLS-scoped client, never service-role.
- `McpRequestLike` (`:22-24`): `{ headers: Record<string, string | undefined> }`.
- `AuthContextResolver` (`:27-29`): `(req: McpRequestLike) => Promise<McpAuthContext>` — **this is the type both API-key and OAuth resolvers must implement.**
- `unimplementedAuthResolver` (`:35-40`): placeholder that throws `McpToolError('unauthenticated', …)`. Exported but not used by the running server (server uses the real API-key resolver).

### `auth/apiKeyResolver.ts`
- `ApiKeyLookup` type (`:17-19`): `keyHash → { userId, orgId } | null`.
- `supabaseApiKeyLookup(anonClient)` (`:27-45`): calls RPC `mcp_resolve_api_key({ p_key_hash })`, maps `row.user_id`/`row.workspace_id` → `{ userId, orgId }`.
- `extractApiKey` (`:61-69`): reads `Authorization: Bearer <key>`, else `x-api-key`.
- `createApiKeyAuthResolver(opts)` (`:71-105`): the resolver factory. Flow: extract key → `hashApiKey(key)` → `opts.lookupKey(hash)` → `mintSupabaseJwt({ sub: userId, orgId }, jwtSecret, tokenTtlSec)` (`:94-98`) → build a Supabase client with `Authorization: Bearer <jwt>` (`:74-80`) → return `{ userId, client, scopes: opts.scopes ?? ['read','write'] }` (`:99-103`). Throws `McpToolError('unauthenticated', …)` at `:85` (missing) and `:92` (invalid/revoked).

### `auth/jwt.ts`
- `hashApiKey(key)` (`:20-23`): SHA-256 hex (matches `apiKeys.ts` convention).
- `SupabaseJwtInput` (`:25-30`): `{ sub, orgId? }`.
- `mintSupabaseJwt(input, secret, ttlSec=300)` (`:36-64`): HS256 via Web Crypto. Claims (`:44-52`): `sub`, `role: 'authenticated'`, `aud: 'authenticated'`, `iss: 'metrimap-mcp'`, `iat`, `exp = now + ttlSec` (default 5 min); and `o: { id: orgId }` only when `orgId` present (`:52`). Throws if secret missing (`:41`).
- `decodeJwtPayload` (`:67-71`): decode-only, no verify (tests/debug).
- **Claim → RLS mapping (confirmed):** `sub` → `public.requesting_user_id()` (`supabase/migrations/20250811100849_remote_schema.sql:127-138`, reads `request.jwt.claims::json->>'sub'`); `o.id` → `public.requesting_org_id()` (`supabase/migrations/20260629150000_workspace_org_scoping.sql:17-20`, reads `...->'o'->>'id'`).

### `index.ts` (module exports)
Barrel at `index.ts:4-38`: registry (`TOOLS`, `listTools`, `dispatchTool`, `McpTool`, `DispatchGuards`), errors (`McpToolError`, `McpErrorCode`), guards (`RateLimiter`, `enforcePayloadSize`, `enforceRateLimit`, `DEFAULT_MAX_PAYLOAD_BYTES`), audit (`noopAuditSink`, `supabaseAuditSink`, `AuditSink`, `McpAuditEntry`), authContext (`unimplementedAuthResolver`, `McpAuthContext`, `McpScope`, `McpRequestLike`, `AuthContextResolver`), and auth (`createApiKeyAuthResolver`, `supabaseApiKeyLookup`, `ApiKeyAuthOptions`, `ApiKeyLookup`, `mintSupabaseJwt`, `hashApiKey`, `decodeJwtPayload`).

### `errors.ts`
`McpErrorCode` (`:3-10`): `invalid_input | unauthenticated | forbidden | not_found | rate_limited | payload_too_large | internal`. `McpToolError extends Error` with `code` + optional `details` (`:12-21`).

### `guards.ts` + `audit.ts` (wrap dispatch)
- `RateLimiter` token bucket (`guards.ts:9-31`); server uses `new RateLimiter(120, 2)` = 120 burst, 2/s, keyed per user (`server.ts:55`; keyed by `ctx.userId` in dispatch).
- `DEFAULT_MAX_PAYLOAD_BYTES = 2_000_000` (`guards.ts:35`); `enforcePayloadSize` (`:37-53`) → `payload_too_large`; `enforceRateLimit` (`:55-63`) → `rate_limited`.
- `audit.ts`: `McpAuditEntry` (`:9-16`), `AuditSink` (`:18-20`), `supabaseAuditSink(client)` (`:26-41`) inserts into `mcp_audit_log` via the caller's RLS client (best-effort). These are injected into dispatch, not wrapped around it externally.

### `registry.ts` — dispatch
- `dispatchTool(name, rawArgs, ctx: McpAuthContext, guards: DispatchGuards = {})` (`:228-288`). Order: payload cap (`:238`) → rate limit keyed on `ctx.userId` (`:239`) → tool lookup / `not_found` (`:241-242`) → **scope check** `tool.scope === 'write' && ctx.scopes && !ctx.scopes.includes('write')` → `forbidden` (`:244-246`) → Zod parse / `invalid_input` (`:264-274`) → run `tool.handler(args, ctx)` (`:277`) → audit ok/error (`:248-262`).
- Each handler builds the API via `api(ctx) = createMetrimapApi(ctx.client, ctx.userId)` (`:41`), i.e. the auth context's client + userId drive all DB access under RLS. 18 tools registered (`TOOLS`, `:47-198`), tagged `read`/`write`.

## 3. Existing OAuth scaffolding

**No functional OAuth code exists — only a typed seam, disabled UI, and docs.** Nothing matched for `PKCE`, `code_verifier`, `WWW-Authenticate`, `/.well-known`, `protected-resource`, `authorization_server`, or a `/register` (DCR) endpoint in code. What exists:

- **Seam/placeholder:** `authContext.ts:26-40` (`AuthContextResolver` type + `unimplementedAuthResolver` referencing "CVS-99 OAuth Connect").
- **Disabled UI:** `ConnectAgentPage.tsx:221-240` — an "Connect with sign-in (OAuth)" section with a "Soon" badge and a `disabled` Connect button.
- **Design doc only:** `docs/features/mcp-server.md:135-142` ("OAuth 'Connect' — follow-up") describes the intended design: an OAuth 2.1 authorization server with dynamic client registration + `/authorize` + `/token`, backed by Clerk sign-in, needing a Clerk OAuth application. Explicitly states it "resolves the same way — a bearer token → user → `mintSupabaseJwt` → RLS-scoped client — so it slots into the same seam."
- Other mentions are narrative only: `docs/features/mcp-connect.md:15`, `mcp-server/README.md:89`, `apiKeyResolver.ts:1-5`, `index.ts:31`.

## 4. App-side API key generation

### `src/shared/lib/supabase/services/apiKeys.ts`
- `randomKey()` (`:25-30`): `mk_live_<48 hex chars>` (24 random bytes).
- `sha256hex()` (`:18-23`): same SHA-256 hex convention as `jwt.ts:hashApiKey` (so lookups line up).
- `createApiKey(name, client)` (`:43-58`): generates key, stores `{ name, key_hash, key_prefix }` (prefix = first 12 chars, `:50`), returns full key **once** (`:57`). No scopes stored.
- `listApiKeys` (`:32-40`) selects `id, name, key_prefix, created_at, last_used_at`; `deleteApiKey` (`:60-64`). All go through `resolveClient(client)` (user RLS client).

### `src/features/settings/pages/ConnectAgentPage.tsx`
"Connect your agent" UI (route `/settings/connect`). Shows: MCP URL (`MCP_URL`, default `https://mcp.canvasm.app/mcp`, overridable via `VITE_MCP_URL`, `:24-26`); a one-time key generator (`generateKey`, `:99-110`); a copy-paste `claude mcp add --transport http metrimap <URL> --header "Authorization: Bearer <key>"` snippet (`:113-117`); claude.ai connector instructions (`:198-208`); a raw `Authorization: Bearer` header for other clients (`:210-219`); the disabled OAuth section (`:221-240`); and a key-count/last-used status (`:242-262`).

## 5. `api_keys` schema + related migrations

### `supabase/migrations/20260630120000_api_keys.sql`
Table `public.api_keys` (`:8-17`): `id uuid PK`, `workspace_id text DEFAULT requesting_org_id()`, `created_by text NOT NULL DEFAULT requesting_user_id()`, `name text`, `key_prefix text`, `key_hash text NOT NULL UNIQUE`, `created_at`, `last_used_at`. **No `scopes` column** (confirmed by grep — per-key scopes are noted as future CVS-89). `public.mcp_resolve_api_key(p_key_hash text)` (`:11-30`): `SECURITY DEFINER`, returns `(user_id text, workspace_id text)`. Rejects hashes < 32 chars (`:18`), bumps `last_used_at` (`:22`), returns owner `created_by` + `workspace_id` for exact hash match (`:24-28`). Granted EXECUTE to `anon, authenticated, service_role` (`:33`) so the pre-auth anon lookup in `apiKeyResolver.ts` works without a service-role key.

### `supabase/migrations/20260704150000_mcp_audit_log.sql`

I have a complete picture. Here is my report.

---

# Clerk ↔ Supabase ↔ MCP integration in Metrimap

Repo explored: `/home/nadeemramli/workspace/github.com/nadeemramli/metrimap-integrations` (its `.env` is a symlink to `../metric-mapping/.env`; `node_modules` is also symlinked — they are the same project).

## Headline answers (a, b, c)

- **(a) Clerk instance/domain:** Production Clerk instance. The publishable key is `pk_live_…`, and decoding it yields the Clerk Frontend API domain **`clerk.canvasm.app`** (app domain `canvasm.app`; MCP planned at `mcp.canvasm.app`). Supabase project ref is **`iqrclwolhookzzmiedun`** (`iqrclwolhookzzmiedun.supabase.co`), per `supabase/migrations/20260626120000_rls_clerk_hardening.sql:3` and `mcp-server/README.md`.
- **(b) Can Clerk issue OAuth access tokens the MCP server could validate?** Not today. The app uses Clerk's **native third-party auth** (session JWTs via `getToken()`), and the MCP server does **not** validate Clerk tokens at all — it authenticates with personal API keys and **mints its own** Supabase HS256 JWT using the shared `SUPABASE_JWT_SECRET`. A true OAuth authorization-server flow backed by Clerk is documented as a **future follow-up (CVS-99)** that would need a **Clerk OAuth application (client id/secret)** which does not exist yet. There is currently **no Clerk secret key** anywhere server-side.
- **(c) How a Clerk user maps to the Supabase RLS identity the tools use:** RLS keys off `public.requesting_user_id()` = the JWT `sub` claim (text), which is the **Clerk user id** (`user_...`). Org/workspace keys off `public.requesting_org_id()` = JWT `o.id`. Any token whose `sub` is the Clerk user id and (optionally) `o.id` is the Clerk org id — whether Clerk-native or MCP-minted — produces the same RLS identity.

---

## 1. Clerk setup

- **ClerkProvider** — `src/main.tsx:1` imports `ClerkProvider` from `@clerk/react-router`; configured at `src/main.tsx:43-48` inside `ThemedClerkProvider`, mounted at `src/main.tsx:96`. Publishable key read at `src/main.tsx:11`: `const PUBLISHABLE_KEY = import.meta.env.VITE_CLERK_PUBLISHABLE_KEY;` (hard-fails if missing, `src/main.tsx:54`).
- **Minting Supabase tokens** — `src/shared/lib/supabase/client.ts:48-70` `createClerkSupabaseClient(getToken)` uses the **native `accessToken()` callback** (no JWT template):
  ```ts
  return createClient<Database>(supabaseUrl, supabaseAnonKey, {
    async accessToken() { return await getToken(); }
  });
  ```
- **`getToken()` call sites** (all return the raw Clerk session JWT, no template arg — the comment at `src/shared/hooks/useClerkSupabase.ts:18-20` explicitly notes requesting a `'supabase'` template would yield `null`):
  - `src/shared/hooks/useClerkSupabase.ts:9,21` (`useAuth().getToken`)
  - `src/features/auth/components/ClerkSupabaseProvider.tsx:16,48` (also upserts the `users` mirror row, `:82`)
  - Consumed app-wide via `src/shared/contexts/AuthenticatedSupabaseContext.tsx` and `useAuthenticatedSupabase()`.

## 2. Clerk user IDs → Supabase RLS

- **Identity function** — `supabase/migrations/20250811100849_remote_schema.sql:124-131`:
  ```sql
  CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION public.requesting_user_id() RETURNS text ... AS $$
    SELECT NULLIF(current_setting('request.jwt.claims', true)::json->>'sub', '')::text;
  $$;
  ```
  (Legacy `get_clerk_user_id()` / `get_current_user_id()` coalesce `sub`/`user_id`/`id` — `…remote_schema.sql:52-88`.)
- **Org function** — `supabase/migrations/20260629150000_workspace_org_scoping.sql:17-21`:
  ```sql
  CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION public.requesting_org_id() RETURNS text ... AS $$
    SELECT NULLIF(current_setting('request.jwt.claims', true)::json->'o'->>'id', '')::text;
  $$;
  ```
  Org role: `supabase/migrations/20260712120000_visibility_rls.sql:31-34` reads `->'o'->>'rol'`.
- **RLS keys off Clerk user id as text — confirmed.** Owner columns are `created_by text` / `user_id text` compared to `requesting_user_id()` throughout, e.g. `api_keys` (`20260630120000_api_keys.sql:10,24`), `spaces`, `metric_values`, `workspace_groups`, `mcp_audit_log`, and `users` (self-row: `…remote_schema.sql`, `id = requesting_user_id()`).
- **Clerk user id format = `user_...`** — confirmed: seed cleanup `DELETE FROM public.users WHERE id IN ('user_AAA','user_BBB','user_CCC')` (`20260626120000_rls_clerk_hardening.sql`), and a real allowlist of prod ids `'user_30ui9qrJF30u0StAHlwM6w2vkHx', 'user_30uQARGezqSApAChPSGSwFyUy85'` at `20250811100849_remote_schema.sql:959`. The `users` mirror row is keyed by the Clerk id (`ClerkSupabaseProvider.tsx:64` upsert `id: user.id`; webhook `clerk-webhook/index.ts` upserts `id: data.id`).

## 3. The Clerk ↔ Supabase JWT (native vs shared-secret)

Two distinct paths coexist:

- **Browser app → native third-party auth (no shared secret in app).** `docs/auth/CLERK_SUPABASE_INTEGRATION.md` and `docs/adr/ADR-002: Using Clerk for User Management.md` state Clerk is registered as a Supabase **third-party auth provider** (Supabase Dashboard → Authentication → Third-party) and the app sends Clerk's own session JWT via `accessToken()`. **No JWT template**, no app-side signing. Supabase verifies the Clerk JWT (via Clerk's JWKS). Note `.env` contains a `SUPABASE_JWKS_URL` var (present but not referenced in repo code — consistent with dashboard-side JWKS config). The third-party config lives in the Supabase **dashboard**, not in repo (`supabase/config.toml` has no `[auth.third_party]` block; none found).
- **MCP server → app mints its OWN Supabase JWT via shared secret.** `src/shared/lib/api/mcp/auth/jwt.ts:33-64` `mintSupabaseJwt({sub, orgId}, secret)` signs **HS256** with the Supabase JWT secret, payload `{ sub: <clerk user id>, role:'authenticated', aud:'authenticated', iss:'metrimap-mcp', o:{id:<orgId>} }`. So MCP tokens are Supabase-issued-style, **not Clerk-issued**. Requires server env **`SUPABASE_JWT_SECRET`** (`mcp-server/server.ts:33-45`, `mcp-server/README.md`, `docs/features/mcp-server.md:144-148`).

## 4. Env / config

Clerk-related env var names (values redacted; from symlinked `.env`, `.env.backup`, `AGENTS.md:128`, `docs/environment/ENVIRONMENT_GUIDE.md`):

- **`VITE_CLERK_PUBLISHABLE_KEY`** — the only Clerk key present; client-side, value is `pk_live_…` (production; decodes to `clerk.canvasm.app`). Dev docs reference `pk_test_…` for a separate dev instance (`ENVIRONMENT_GUIDE.md:42,54,125`).
- **No Clerk secret key anywhere.** No `CLERK_SECRET_KEY` / `CLERK_API_KEY` / frontend-API URL var in `.env`, `.env.example`, code, or docs. The only other Clerk secret is **`CLERK_WEBHOOK_SIGNING_SECRET`** (a Svix `whsec_…` webhook signature secret, **not** a Clerk API/OAuth credential), consumed only in the edge function `supabase/functions/clerk-webhook/index.ts:52` via `Deno.env.get(...)` (Supabase Edge secret, not in the repo `.env`).
- Supabase vars in `.env`: `VITE_SUPABASE_URL`, `VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY`, `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL`, `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY`, `SUPABASE_URL`, `SUPABASE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY`, `SUPABASE_SECRET_KEY`, `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY`, `SUPABASE_JWKS_URL`, `SUPABASE_ACCESS_TOKEN`, `SUPABASE_PROJECT_REF`, `SUPABASE_DB_PASSWORD`, `DATABASE_URL`. Note **`SUPABASE_JWT_SECRET` is not in the current `.env`** — it is required only at MCP deploy time (`mcp-server/README.md`).
- MCP app env: `VITE_MCP_URL` (defaults to `https://mcp.canvasm.app/mcp`, `src/features/settings/pages/ConnectAgentPage.tsx:22-24`).

## 5. Existing OAuth / "sign in with" / M2M usage with Clerk

- **API-key (M2M) path is built and shipped** (this is what the MCP tools use today):
  - Resolver `src/shared/lib/api/mcp/auth/apiKeyResolver.ts` — extracts `Authorization: Bearer <key>` (or `x-api-key`), SHA-256 hashes it (`jwt.ts:hashApiKey`), looks up owner via RPC, then `mintSupabaseJwt` → RLS-scoped client. Owner lookup RPC `public.mcp_resolve_api_key` (`supabase/migrations/20260704160000_mcp_resolve_api_key.sql`) returns `(user_id, workspace_id)`.
  - Seam definition `src/shared/lib/api/mcp/authContext.ts` (`McpAuthContext { userId, client, scopes }`); server wiring `mcp-server/server.ts:47-52`.
  - Key generation UI `src/features/settings/pages/ConnectAgentPage.tsx` (route `/settings/connect`) and `src/shared/lib/supabase/services/apiKeys.ts`; keys stored hashed in `public.api_keys` (`20260630120000_api_keys.sql`).
- **OAuth "Connect / sign in with Clerk" is NOT implemented** — it is an explicit follow-up:
  - `ConnectAgentPage.tsx:212-235` renders a disabled "Connect with sign-in (OAuth)" button labeled "Soon".
  - `docs/features/mcp-server.md:135-142`: the plan is an "OAuth 2.1 authorization server (dynamic client registration + `/authorize` + `/token`) backed by Clerk sign-in… Needs a Clerk **OAuth application** (client id/secret, redirect URIs). Ship API-key auth first."
  - `authContext.ts:32-40` `unimplementedAuthResolver` throws "MCP auth is not wired yet — implement the AuthContextResolver in CVS-99 (OAuth Connect + personal API key)."
- No other third-party "sign in with" or Clerk M2M/OAuth token usage exists in the codebase.

### Implication for the MCP server
Because RLS only needs a JWT with `sub` = Clerk user id (+ optional `o.id`), the MCP server can adopt Clerk as an OAuth IdP two ways: (i) **validate Clerk-issued OAuth access tokens** (would require creating a Clerk OAuth application and obtaining Clerk's issuer/JWKS — none configured yet, no Clerk secret key present), then re-mint a Supabase JWT via the existing `mintSupabaseJwt` seam; or (ii) keep the current design and just map the Clerk-authenticated `sub` into `mintSupabaseJwt`. The mapping to the tools' RLS identity is already solved by `apiKeyResolver.ts` + `jwt.ts` — the only missing piece for a Clerk-OAuth path is a Clerk OAuth application and a token-verification step slotting into `AuthContextResolver`.

## Headline answers (a, b, c)

- **(a) Clerk instance/domain:** Production Clerk instance. The publishable key is `pk_live_…`, and decoding it yields the Clerk Frontend API domain **`clerk.canvasm.app`** (app domain `canvasm.app`; MCP planned at `mcp.canvasm.app`). Supabase project ref is **`iqrclwolhookzzmiedun`** (`iqrclwolhookzzmiedun.supabase.co`), per `supabase/migrations/20260626120000_rls_clerk_hardening.sql:3` and `mcp-server/README.md`.
